entries.full_title: English Cookery Books to the Year 1850
entries.short_title: English Cookery Books to the Year 1850
entries.short_note: This work presents a list of title-pages of English books on cookery and domestic economy up to the year 1850. In the prefatory note the author explains that an earlier book, Notes from a Collector’s Catalogue, listed English cookery books to 1700 with library locations and collations of editions. He states that his primary interest lies in the historical combination of cookery with medicine, common in early books and persisting into the early nineteenth century before declining.

entries.full_title: Host and Guest
Publishers’ Weekly (New York), vol. vi, 388, 421. Two lists by H. W. Hageman.
entries.volumes_overview: Serial/periodical reference; volume VI is cited (pages 388 and 421).

entries.full_title: A Book about the Table
entries.volumes_overview: Multi-volume work; the bibliography cites volume 2, chapter 17.

entries.full_title: Bibliographie Gastronomique
Old Cookery Books
National Review, vol. 24, 676; 25, 776. Two papers by Col. A. Kenney Herbert.
entries.volumes_overview: Two papers in the National Review, cited in volumes 24 and 25 (pages 676 and 776).

entries.full_title: The Pleasures of the Table
My Cookery Books
A Book of Simples
Notes from a Collector’s Catalogue with a bibliography of English cookery books
THIS IS THE BOKE OF COKERY
entries.short_title: This is the Boke of Cokery
entries.short_note: Here beginneth a noble boke of festes royalle and Cokery a boke for a pryncis housholde or any otherestates ; and the makynge therof as ye shall fynde more playnly within this boke.The imprint states that it was printed by Richard Pynson in the year MD (1500) 'without temple barre'. The bibliography notes that the title is given in Ames and Herbert, who report that the Dowager Duchess of Portland brought the book from Bulstrode to London for Herbert to examine. A similar title is recorded by Maunsell as printed by John Byddell, likely the same work, suggesting a later printing window between 1533 and 1540.This title is given in Ames and Herbert,[^1] where it is stated thatthe Dowager Duchess of Portland brought the book from Bulstrode to London for Herbert to examine. A book called ‘A noble boke of feastes royall, and of Cookerìe, for Princes housholde, or any other estate, and the making thereof’, is given by Maunsell [^2] as having been printed by John Byddell. This is doubtless the same book, and the date would be between 1533 and 1540.

entries.full_title: HERE BEGYNNĒTH THE BOKE OF KERVYNGE
entries.short_note: This book is in the Cambridge Library. The colophon describes a manual of 'servyce and kervynge and sewynge' and household offices for a prince or other estate, together with the feasts in the year. Copies are noted with colophons dated 1508 and 1513, each printed by Wynkyn de Worde at London in Fleet Street at the sign of the sun. The work outlines specialized carving terms, gives the butler’s duties with a list of wines, and sets out detailed responsibilities for the chamberlain, marshal, and usher regarding household order and precedence. It was later reprinted (including by Abraham Veale and by E. Allde, with a dated 1613 issue), and was also incorporated into other household/cookery compilations; it was edited for the Early English Text Society in 1868 by F. W. Furnivall.

entries.full_title: THE TREASURE OF POORE MEN
entries.short_note: Described as a small book of 44 leaves, it includes recipes for aqua vitae and claret but is mainly medical in character.

entries.full_title: THE CASTEL OF HELTHE
entries.short_note: Although included in this context, it is explicitly described as not being a cookery book. It offers advice on the uses and physical effects of different articles of diet. The note adds that, according to the Dictionary of National Biography, Elyot incurred the wrath of physicians for encroaching on their domain.

entries.full_title: A PROPER NEWE BOOKE OF COKERYE, declarynge what maner of meates be beste in season, for all times in the yere, and how they ought to be dressed, and served at the table, both for fleshe dayes, and fyshe dayes. With a new addition, verye necessarye for all them that delyghteth in Cokerye
entries.short_title: A Proper Newe Booke of Cokerye
entries.short_note: The earliest known edition is described as undated and held in the library of Corpus Christi College, Cambridge, printed by John Kynge and Thomas Marche; it is a small book of 16 leaves (A to B in eights, with B8 blank). Further editions are reported by bibliographers, including an edition of 1545 and another of 1546, and later British Museum copies dated 1575 and 1576. The note suggests that similarly titled entries in Maunsell and Ames and Herbert may represent editions of the same work. Examples of unusual recipes are listed, including tarts of borage flowers and marigolds, and 'To make egges in mone shine.'

entries.full_title: "THE SECRETES OF THE REVERENDE MAISTER ALEXIS OF PIEMONT.
entries.short_title: The Secretes of Alexis of Piemont
entries.short_note: Containing excellente remedies against divers diseases, woundes, and other accidents, with the manner to make distilations, parfumes, confitures, ditynges, colours, fusions and meltynges. A worke well approved, verye proffytable and necessary for every man. A practical compilation of remedies and techniques including distillations, perfumes, and confectionery-related preparations, presented as a useful work for many purposes. The 1558 edition is explicitly stated to be translated out of French into English by Wyllyam Warde. The imprint names John Kingstone as printer in London for the bookseller Nicolas Inglande, dwelling in Poules Churchyarde, dated November 1558.

entries.full_title: A COMPEDYOUS REGYMENT OR A DYETARY OF HELTH
entries.short_title: A Compedyous Regyment or a Dyetary of Helth
entries.short_note: The work is described as containing recipes for making ipocras, confitures, etc., but it is characterized primarily as a health and diet regimen rather than a cookery book. Like Elyot’s Castle of Health, it offers advice on the use of food. The earliest edition is said to have no date, while the next edition is dated 1562. The author is noted as a physician with varied experiences and a reputation described as not always reputable, and it is stated that he had once been a bishop.

entries.full_title: THE TREASURIE OF COMMODIOUS CONCEITES, AND HIDDEN SECRETS
entries.short_title: The Good Huswives Closet (Treasurie of Commodious Conceites)
entries.short_note: Commonly called The Good Huswives Closet of provision for the health of her household, the book is presented as a collection of practical 'experiments' for household health and related preparations. The earliest known copy is missing its title-page, but a 1573 colophon is quoted. The text emphasizes conserves and their virtues, and includes cosmetic and medicinal recipes (e.g., to sweeten the breath, to make hair yellow, to make Manus Christi), as well as powders and preparations for elite households. It is dedicated to Master Richard Wiston of the Company of the Barbers and Surgeons, and its price is stated to be four pence. The work later appears in quarto (1600) under a somewhat different title and was repeatedly reprinted through the seventeenth century.

entries.full_title: A DIRECTION FOR THE HEALTH OF MAGISTRATES AND STUDENTES
entries.short_title: A Direction for the Health of Magistrates and Studentes
entries.short_note: The work is explicitly described as not being a cookery book, but as giving advice on the use of food. It was written in Latin by Gulielmus Gratarolus and translated into English by T. N. (identified in the text as Thomas Newton). Gratarolus is described as an Italian who left his country after embracing the Calvinist faith, later practising physic at Basel, and dying in 1568. The translator Thomas Newton is described as Rector of Little Ilford. The imprint specifies London, Fleet Street, with William How printing for Abraham Veale in 1574.

entries.full_title: A COMPENDIUM OF THE RATIONALL SECRETES of the worthie Knight and moste excellent Doctour of Phisicke and Chirurgerie, Leonardo Phioravante Bolognese, divided into three Bookes
entries.short_title: A Compendium of the Rationall Secretes
entries.short_note: The book is divided into three internal books: the first on secrets pertaining to physic, the second on secrets pertaining to surgery, and the third on compositions for physic and surgery, including the virtues of vegetables, animals, and minerals. The cookery receipts are said to be in Book III. It was printed in London in 1582 by Jhon Kyngston for George Pen and I. H. The author is said to have died at Bologna in 1588. The translator is identified as John Hester, and an example of the book’s 'curious' content is quoted involving treating a capon with nettles to make it call chickens.

entries.full_title: THE SCHOOLEMASTER or Teacher of Table Philosophie
entries.short_title: The Schoolemaster; or Teacher of Table Philosophie
entries.short_note: The book is described as a companion to guide moderate and wholesome diet and to provide honest mirth and 'delectable devises'. It is organized into four treatises: on the nature and quality of meats, drinks, and sauces; on manners and behavior at table; on pleasant questions for entertainment; and on merry honest jests and devices. It was printed in London by Richard Johnes in 1583. The authorship is presented as uncertain in the text, attributing it either to Thomas Twyne (a doctor of Lewes) or to Thomas Turswell (a canon of St Paul’s), and it is noted that the D.N.B. mentions an edition of 1576.

entries.full_title: THE GOOD HUSWIFES JEWELL.
entries.short_note: This household book includes 'conceits in cookery' alongside instructions for distilling waters, making oils, and providing medicines for diseases, plus points of husbandry. Although a date of 1585 is queried in the heading, the text states that no earlier edition than 1596 seems known, while other bibliographers mention an undated edition and an edition of 1610. It is described as a small 12° of 52 leaves with the head-line 'A Booke of Cookerie'. The work was later reprinted in 1620 under the title A Booke of Cookerie. Examples of dishes mentioned include muggets, a mortis, and blewmanger, and a remedy for obesity based on fennel is quoted.

entries.full_title: THE SECOND PART OF THE GOOD HUSWIFES JEWELL
entries.short_title: The Second Part of the Good Huswifes Jewell
entries.short_note: This book presents methods for distilling sweet waters, preserving fruits, and making syrups, along with cookery conceits 'after the Italian and French maner'. It was printed in London for Edward White in 1585. The text notes that an edition of 1597 added 'The Book of Carving', with the carving and sewing section having a separate title-page and being unpaged. Other editions are reported for 1590 and 1606. The book is said to be similar to the first part and to have 72 pages, with culinary recipes such as 'Pursse or Cremitaries', 'Jombrils', 'Colluce', and 'Condonacke'.

entries.full_title: THE OLDE MANS DIETARIE.
entries.short_note: This work is described as a learned guide for preserving old persons in perfect health and soundness. It is an English translation from Latin, first published in English by Thomas Newton. Like Gratarolus’s work (also translated by Newton), it is explicitly said not to be a cookery book but to give advice on the use of foods. It was printed in London for Edward White at the sign of the Gun in 1586.

entries.full_title: THE HOUSEHOLDERS PHILOSOPHIE
entries.short_note: Wherein is perfectly & profitably described, the true Oeconomia and forme of Housekeeping, with a Table added there- unto of all the notable things therein contained. This is a set of rules and regulations ; there are no recipes. The original, *Il Padre di Famiglia*, was published in 1583

entries.full_title: Il Padre di Famiglia
entries.short_note: An English translation (by “T. K.”) of Torquato Tasso’s Italian work, issued in London in 1588. The book includes an appended “dairic Booke for all good hus-wives.” The description here characterizes the work as a set of household rules and regulations rather than a recipe collection. The Italian original is stated to have been published in 1583.

entries.full_title: The Good Hous-wives Treasurie
entries.short_note: Being a verye necessarie Booke instructing to the dressing of Meates. Hereunto is also annexed sundrie holsome Medicines for divers diseases. A small duodecimo printed in London in 1588. It is described as providing cookery recipes (e.g., “How to make a Florentine,” “How to make Dousets,” “For a Tansie,” and “How to make a Cullis for a sick body”). The volume also includes “sundrie holsome Medicines for divers diseases.”

entries.full_title: A BOOK OF COOKRYE
entries.short_note: Very necessary for all such as delight therin. Gathered by A. W. And now newlye enlarged, with the serving in of the Table. With the proper Sauces to each of them convenient. Printed in London in 1591 and attributed to “A. W.” It is noted that a copy of the 1591 edition is in the Bodleian and that a 1594 reprint is in the B.M. The description highlights a range of recipes, including “To boyle a Cony with a pudding in his belly,” “To still a Cocke for a weake body that is consumed,” and novelty puddings and pies (e.g., pudding in a turnip root, pudding in a cucumber, and a tart of brier hips).

entries.full_title: THE GOOD HUSWIFES HANDMAIDE FOR THE KITCHIN
entries.short_title: The Good Huswifes Handmaide for the Kitchin
entries.short_note: Containing manie principall pointes of Cookerie, as well how to dresse meates, after sundrie the best fashions used in England and other Countries, with their apt and proper sawces, both for flesh and fish, as also the orderly serving of the same to the Table. Hereunto are annexed, sundrie necessarie Conceits for the preservation of health. Verie meete to be adjoined to the good Huswifes Closet of provision for her Houshold.A duodecimo cookery and household book printed in London in 1594 and described as a “little 12° of 55 leaves,” with a copy in the Bodleian. It includes instructions on dressing meats and fish, suitable sauces, and the orderly serving of dishes, plus “conceits” for preserving health. According to Maunsell, a first edition was printed in 1588 by Richard Jones and Ant. Hyll, and the head-line reads “A New Booke of Cookerie.” The text notes detailed preserving practices (including three methods for keeping lard associated with named aristocratic households) and recipes such as a “tart to provoke courage,” “Restons,” “a Vaunt,” and “fine manchet.” A 1597 issue is said to be the same book with a new title.

entries.full_title: THE WIDDOWES TREASURE
entries.short_note: A mixed collection of medical and household material printed in London in 1595, combining “secrets” in physic and surgery with cookery practices and remedies, including for diseases in cattle. The preface is signed “M. R.” and claims the work was written at the request of a gentlewoman in the country for private use and that her name would be remembered by posterity; the description expresses skepticism about this claim given the frank treatment of certain diseases. The contents are described as unarranged, with topics ranging from preserving venison and pears to killing lice and whitening linen. Editions of 1631 and 1639 are noted; the 1631 edition is said to be in the Bodleian.

entries.full_title: A THOUSAND NOTABLE THINGS of sundry sorts, &c.
entries.short_note: Attributed to Thomas Lupton and described as almost entirely medical. The text notes that there are many editions, including one as late as 1815. The earliest edition mentioned is undated but said to be about 1595, printed in London by John Charlewood for Hughe Spooner, whose shop is given in Lumbard Street at the sign of the Cradle.

entries.full_title: EPULARIO, or The Italian Banquet: Wherein is shewed the maner how to dresse and prepare all kind of Flesh, Foules, or Fishes. As also how to make Sauces, Tartes, Pies, etc. After the maner of all Countries. With an addition of many other profitable and necessary things.
entries.short_note: An English translation printed in London in 1598, stated here to be translated from an Italian book by Giovanne de Rosselli. The note adds (citing Brunet) that the Italian original first appeared in 1516. The description highlights striking recipes including a pie designed so that live birds fly out when it is cut, and another for pies made with combs of cocks and hens (including stones and livers) combined with cherries, cinnamon, ginger, saffron, and verjuice.

entries.full_title: DYETS DRY DINNER:
entries.short_note: Printed in London in 1599 and authored by Henry Buttes, M.A., Fellow of C.C.C. in C.Q. The text emphasizes that it is not a cookery book: it describes different foodstuffs with a “story for table-talke” about each, arranged on facing sides of the page. Biographical details are supplied, including that Buttes was elected master of his college in 1626 and vice-chancellor in 1629, and that he was found hanged in 1632; a portrait is said to exist in the master’s lodge, and a reported portrait in Harding’s Biographical Mirror is dismissed as likely mistaken.

entries.full_title: NATURALL AND ARTIFICIAL DIRECTIONS for health, derived from the best Philosophers, as well moderne, as auncient.
entries.short_title: Naturall and Artificial Directions for health
entries.short_note: A health and regimen work by William Vaughan, printed in London in 1600; part of it concerns the use of foods. The author’s life dates are given as 1577–1641, and he is described as having helped to colonize Newfoundland. Subsequent editions are listed as 1602 (2), 1607 (3), 1617 (5), 1626 (6), and 1633 (7).

entries.full_title: DELIGHTES FOR LADIES, To adorne their Persons, Tables, Closets, and Distillatories; with Beauties, Banquets, Perfumes, & Waters. Reade, Practice, & Censure.
entries.short_note: A “most charming little book” (described as within decorative borders woven with the initials “E R”), printed at London by Peter Short; the year is given with uncertainty. Its collation is described as “A to H in twelves,” with H12 a blank leaf with borders, and a rhymed epistle signed “H. Plat.” The work was reprinted many times (including 1602, 1608, 1609, 1611, 1615, 1630, 1632, and 1636), and later editions (1647, 1651, 1654, 1656, 1683) are noted as being printed without the borders; Hazlitt is said to list editions of 1600, 1603, 1618, and 1640. Biographical information is included: Hugh Plat, son of a London brewer, was born in 1552 and knighted in 1605.

entries.full_title: THE ENGLISHMAN’S DOCTER, OR THE SCHOOLE OF SALERNE, or, Physicall observations for the perfect Preserving of the body of Man in continuall Health.
entries.short_note: Described as the earliest English translation of a famous Latin poem attributed to the physicians of Salerno, written for the preservation of the health of Robert, eldest son of William the Conqueror. The accompanying commentary is attributed to Arnaldus de Villa Nova (d. c. 1313). The commentary had been translated as early as 1528 by T. Paynell. The poem focuses on the use of foods for health. A later edition of 1634 is noted as having an added discourse on fish used in England and their effects on health.

entries.full_title: A CLOSET FOR LADIES AND GENTLEWOMEN, or, The Art of preserving, Conserving, and Candying. With the manner howe to make divers kinds of Syrups : and all kind of banquetting stuffes. Also divers soveraigne Medicines and Salves, for sundry Diseases.
entries.short_title: A Closet for Ladies and Gentlewomen
entries.short_note: A small English book on preserving, conserving, candying, syrups, banquetting “stuffes,” and household medicines and salves. It is described as resembling Hugh Plat’s Delightes for Ladies, and is attributed to Plat in the Douce catalogue, though the text suggests it appears more like a rival work. The book is noted for numerous appealing recipes, including novelty items (e.g., a walnut concealing biscuits and caraways) and several marmalades, one said to have been given to Queen Mary as a New Year’s gift. It was frequently reprinted across the 17th century, with changes in decorative borders, the addition/removal of a portrait frontispiece, and later inclusion of a table of contents.

entries.full_title: THE PHILOSOPHERS BANQUET ; newly furnished and decked forth with much variety of many severall Dishes, that in the former Service were neglected. Where now not onely Meates and Drinks of all Natures and Kindes are served in, but the Names and Kindes of all disputed of. The second Edition, newly corrected and inlarged, to almost as much more. By W. B. Esquire.
entries.short_note: This is the second edition (1614) of a work entered in the Stationers’ Register in 1609, though no copy of that first edition is known. It discusses the uses of foods alongside many other topics, and is noted as the first book to contain a quotation from Hamlet. A third edition is reported for 1633 (printed for Nicholas Vavasour). The underlying Latin work Mensa Philosophica (first known edition 1475) is stated to have been ascribed to Sir Michael Scott and also to Theobald Anguilbert, an Irish physician. A Sotheby’s note suggests W. B. may be the same person associated with several other publications, and that part of A Help to Discourse derives from this book.

entries.full_title: THE ENGLISH HUS-WIFE, Contayning, The inward and outward vertues which ought to be in a compleat woman. As, her skill in Physicke, Cookery, Banqueting-stuffe, Distillation, Perfumes, Wooll, Hemp, Flax, Davries, Brewing, Baking, and all other things belonging to an Houshould. A Worke very profitable and necessarie, gathered for the generall good of this kingdome.
entries.short_note: Presented as the second book of Countrey Contentments by Gervase Markham (c. 1568–1637), described here as “the earliest English hackney writer.” The work covers a wide range of household arts, including cookery, banqueting, distillation, brewing, baking, and domestic materials such as wool, hemp, and flax. The cookery is described as more modern than in earlier books though it retains obsolete dishes (e.g., leach and jumbal). The medical recipes are characterized as particularly unpleasant, with animal dung and other filth frequently used. Numerous later editions are listed, including a 1907 reprint by Constance, Countess De La Warr.

entries.full_title: A DAILY EXERCISE FOR LADIES AND GENTLEWOMEN. Whereby they may learne and practise the whole Art of making Pastes, Preserves, Marmalades, Conserves, Tart-stuffes, Gellies, Breads, Sucket-Candies, Cordiall waters, Conceits in Sugar-workes of several kindes. As also to dry Lemonds, Orenges, or other Fruits. Newly set forth, according to the now approved Receipts, used both by Honourable and Worshipfull personages. By John Murrel Professor thereof.
entries.short_title: A Daily Exercise for Ladies and Gentlewomen
entries.short_note: A duodecimo-format recipe book aimed at ladies and gentlewomen, teaching pastes, preserves, marmalades, conserves, tart-stuffes, gellies, breads, sucket-candies, cordial waters, and sugar-work “conceits,” as well as drying citrus and other fruits. It is described as printed within borders and includes a table of contents and a short preface advertising the sale of moulds used for the book’s specified forms. The recipes range from medicinal items (e.g., “Paste of Regia against a Consumption”) to numerous confections and marmalades. It is described as very rare; the narrator reports buying a copy at Sotheby’s in 1904 for £10 5s.

entries.full_title: A NEW BOOK OF COOKERIE. Wherein is set forth a most perfect direction to furnish an extraordinary, or ordinary feast, either in Summer or Winter. Also a Bill of Fare for Fish-dayes, Fasting dayes, Ember-weekes, or Lent. And likewise the most commendable fashion of Dressing, or Sowcing, either Flesh, Fish, or Fowle : for making of Gellies, and other Made-dishes for service, to beautifie either Noble-mans or Gentlemans Table. Together with the newest fashion of cutting up any Fowle. Hereunto also is added the most exquisite English Cookerie. All set forth according to the now, new, English and French fashion. By John Murrell.
entries.short_note: An English cookery book offering directions for arranging both extraordinary and ordinary feasts in summer or winter, plus bills of fare for fish-days, fasting days, ember weeks, and Lent. It includes methods for dressing and “sowcing” meats, fish, and fowl, instructions for gellies and other made-dishes, and guidance on carving. The book is said to begin with dishes for various courses at a feast, and it highlights several recipes presented as new (e.g., French-style “smooring” of coney/duck/mallard, very crisp pancakes, a salad of rose-buds and clove gilly-flowers, and puddings described as “fierced” and “fond”).

entries.full_title: A BOOKE OF COOKERIE. And the order of Meates to bee served to the Table, both for Flesh and Fish dayes. With many excellent wayes for the dressing of all usuall sortes of meates, both Bak’t, boyld, or rosted, of Flesh, fish, Fowle, or others, with their proper sauces. As also many rare Inventions in Cookery for made Dishes; with most notable preserves of sundry sorts of Fruits. Likewise for making many precious Waters, with divers approved Medicines for grievous diseases. With certaine points of Husbandry how to Order Oxen, Horses, Sheepe, Hogges, &c., with many other necessary points for Husbandmen to know.
entries.short_note: A broad household and cookery manual covering table service for flesh and fish days, preparation methods (baked, boiled, roasted) for meats, fish, and fowl, and their sauces. It also includes “made dishes,” fruit preserves, the making of “precious waters,” and medicinal remedies, along with points of husbandry on managing livestock and other practical matters for husbandmen. The text states that it is merely an abbreviated edition of Thomas Dawson’s work (title not fully given in the excerpt).

entries.full_title: Huswifes Jewell
VIA RECTA AD VITAM LONGAM . . .
entries.short_note: The text states that this work is a book on the uses of foods. It is attributed to Tobias Venner, described as a Doctor of Physicke. The author is said to have lived from 1577 to 1660. The excerpt lists multiple editions (including 1620, 1622, 1628, 1638, 1650, and 1660).

entries.full_title: A DELIGHTFULL DAILY EXERCISE FOR LADIES AND GENTLEWOMEN.. Whereby is set foorth the secrete misteries of the purest preservings in Glasses and other Confrictionaries, as making of Breads, Pastes, Preserves, Suckets, Marmalates, Tartstuffes, rough Candies, and many other things never before in Print. Whereto is added a Booke of Cookery.
entries.short_title: A Delightfull Daily Exercise for Ladies and Gentlewomen
